concept: Die / Death / The Ending of a Life
description: To die. The second threshold of the life-arc, partnered with 'thowia' (born/birth). Deliberately kin to 'lumae' (end): dying named as a kind of ending, within the same gentle sound-family, so the word is unblinking about what it names while carrying no harshness.
sound_symbolism: The liquid 'lu' flows downward into depth, 'me' settles like breath growing quiet, and the final 'o' closes the circle — whole, complete, rounded shut. Nothing sharp, nothing violent: a life arriving at its own edge. The 'lum-' opening it shares with 'lumae' (end) teaches the word's meaning through family resemblance.
grammatical_notes: Verb: 'shia to lumeo.' (She died.) With a plural subject: 'lo shia to lumeo.' (They died.) The noun, death, comes by the event-noun rule and can stand as subject: 'lumeo wei theula lioru shua.' (Death comes to all life.) With grieving: 'mia nuhemi. ni melu to lumeo.' (I grieve. A dear friend died.)

pillars.secular-buddhist-philosophy: The contemplation of death is the heart of impermanence practice. A language whose Buddhist pillar is real must be able to say 'die' plainly — euphemism is a form of looking away, and 'lumeo' does not look away.
pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: Grief that can be spoken plainly does not have to become anger. Plain words for death make honest mourning — and honest condolence — possible.
pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: Traditional cultures kept death visible: washed, sung, buried by hand. Naming it directly is older and kinder than hiding it.

semantic_domains.nature: The universal ending of living things
semantic_domains.ritual: The event around which mourning gathers
semantic_domains.temporal: The second threshold of a life
